total_aligned_gt.meeting_context: City Visual Arts Committee reviewed minutes, public art project updates, and the annual work plan; discussed upcoming September agenda items and a robust set of public art strategy initiatives. The committee also advanced public art inventory/mapping efforts, considered economic impact data to support strategy, and aligned the 2024 Center for Performing Arts (CPA) gallery call and schedule.

points.speaker: Jesse Cupp
points.text: Meeting minutes were approved unanimously following a motion by Cliff Grant and a second by Jesse Cupp.
points.source: Jesse Cupp : OK, unanimous.

topics.topic: North Bayshore/Shoreline Master Plan Community Spaces

points.speaker: Susi Merhar
points.text: The North Bayshore Master Plan was approved and includes 55,000 square feet for community spaces. Susi Merhar proposed proactively aligning the Visual Arts Strategy to recommend artist studios, galleries, and makerspaces.
points.source: Susi Merhar : Maste plan was approved, right. And I read that there's 55,000 square feet for. Community spaces.

points.speaker: John Lang
points.text: John Lang confirmed nothing is decided yet and a process will determine specific uses, suggesting capturing broader policy recommendations so development review can designate arts-related community spaces.
points.source: John Lang : So it's one all this still needs to go through any review, building review to build. So it it's going to be a ways off, but it does create that framework of setting aside space to activate for me.

points.speaker: John Lang
points.text: September agenda will include: Public Works briefing on Castro Street interim pedestrian mall street treatments; Evelyn Mini Park call for artists; transit grade separation art second-round evaluations; and Rengstorff Park tennis/maintenance building mural concepts narrowing, with artists invited to present.
points.source: John Lang : Coming back in September you're going to have a very full agenda. So two items in particular that are going to be on your agenda, I don't want to flag for you that are you're not aware of today is one will be a conversation on street treatment of Castro Street in the central corridor for the pedestrian mall.

topics.topic: Capital Improvement Program Public Art Updates

points.speaker: John Lang
points.text: Aquatic Center: No new update. Public Works engineering continues coordinating with all three selected artists to prepare their projects for installation.
points.source: John Lang : So, so the first one is the Aquatic Center, not any new update there. Public works engineer continues to work with all three artists on getting their projects prepped for installation.

points.speaker: John Lang
points.text: Fayette Park: Artist Colin Seelig has completed all three functional art pieces (including a squirrel and a lizard) and is ready to install. The City is finishing pad preparation to enable installation in the park.
points.source: John Lang : The artist Colin Seelig has actually this is one picture he sent, but he has now completed all three of the functional art pieces and he's actually ready to install. So we're just on the City side trying to wrap up the pads to get this installed in the park.

points.speaker: John Lang
points.text: Rengstorff Park maintenance and tennis buildings: Agreements with artists Fernanda and Harumo are executed. Each was asked to produce three concept renderings for September committee consideration, with small stipends provided.
points.source: John Lang : Is on rank store park, maintenance and tennis buildings. As I mentioned, agreements with Fernanda and Harumo have been executed and have asked them to complete 3 concept designs for consideration at your September meeting.

points.speaker: John Lang
points.text: Transit Grade Separation Access Project: Five of six artists are under contract; the sixth has a W-9 issue being resolved but can meet the deadline. Deliverables (renderings or 3D models) are due July 21, after which Public Works will pre-screen; the committee will score and discuss selections in September.
points.source: John Lang : Five of the six artists are under contract. Sixth. We had a small issue with their W 9, which we're getting resolved, which should not impact this deliverable, but they have till July 21st either come up with renderings

topics.topic: Public Art Inventory and Mapping Progress

points.speaker: Susi Merhar
points.text: The subcommittee is updating a comprehensive inventory of public and publicly accessible private artworks, reorganized by year with older pieces at top and newer ones at bottom. Notable clusters were documented around Google's Charleston East campus, including six Burning Man sculptures.
points.source: Susi Merhar : And right now it's, it's I I ordered by by year, right. You can just let me call them whatever. So before it was by map number that was just easier because that that's how I put it in. But now it's by year. So we have the older pieces on top and the new ones at the bottom.

points.speaker: Cliff Grant
points.text: Two previously cataloged artworks were found missing, including a city-commissioned piece reported disappeared around 1997, and another likely relocated during construction near Google. This gap underscores the need for a formal deaccession process and clearer handling of private art relocations.
points.source: Cliff Grant : There's 2 2 I didn't find 2 pieces that were on the original spreadsheet and I was able to contact the artist of one who mentioned that in 1997 he was told it had disappeared.

topics.topic: Economic Impact Research for Strategy Support

points.speaker: Jesse Cupp
points.text: Research sources identified to quantify arts' local economic impact: SV Creates' 2021 study and Americans for the Arts AEP5 (2017), with AEP6 forthcoming. Plan is to use 2017 spending figures (e.g., $26.27 average per attendee, higher for non-residents) and local attendance to estimate impacts via the AEP calculator.
points.source: Jesse Cupp : These numbers that show that nonprofits, parts and cultural events, the average attendee spends $26.27 per person. So the cost of admission and then it's also broken down into different categories, transportation, food, etcetera and it's broken down into if. You're a non. Resident You spend more money. You spend $35 on average.

topics.topic: Annual Work Plan Update and Approval

points.speaker: John Lang
points.text: Staff revised the annual work plan to specify the council study session timing for the Public Art Strategy; retitle and refocus the utility box program under Sidewalk Studio Phase 2; and clarify collaboration language with the Chamber.
points.source: John Lang : Item number one, the development of a public art strategy being more defiant definitive of when that would go to council just kind of looking at the dates that were available that aren't already planned study sessions.

points.speaker: Susi Merhar
points.text: The committee adjusted Item 4 to emphasize promoting the Public Art Strategy, with milestones to update the 1996 public art book, refresh the online map, and update the website content. The work plan was then approved unanimously with this change.
points.source: Susi Merhar : I was just wondering as a title, as this description, it would work. Publish, you know, promote the public art strategy. And then the key milestones would be doing the art book, doing the online map and updating our website.

points.speaker: Susi Merhar
points.text: Members proposed expanding goals to explicitly include diversity, representation, public participation, and art appreciation, and to broaden collaboration beyond arts organizations to include businesses, schools, residents, and government bodies.
points.source: Susi Merhar : It it's not that we're missing it but in the goals we talk only community, place making heritage and economic development, we're leaving out diversity. We're leaving out like our depreciation, just the value of reflection, just as art as right, confrontation, reflection, all of those things.

topics.topic: Center for Performing Arts 2024 Gallery Call

points.speaker: John Lang
points.text: To realign with CPA's scheduling cycle, the 2024 gallery call for artists will proceed without a theme. Display space now includes the second floor for more pieces. Staff will return in October to finalize selections and restore a summer call timeline for future years.
points.source: John Lang : So last late last year we did the call for artists for 2023, but normally the call for artists for the CPA is done during the summertime, so all the agreements next year are done in the fall. Not so the way we're off by about five months.

points.speaker: Jesse Cupp
points.text: Meeting adjourned at 8:14 PM.
points.source: Jesse Cupp : So then it is 814 and this meeting is adjourned. Thank you for coming.

points.speaker: John Lang
points.text: John Lang committed to email Jasmin Aber to connect offline and explain VAC processes and engagement options.
points.source: John Lang : So Jasmine, I stopped recording, but just it might be good if you and I chatted just so you can understand how the VAC works and how better to engage.

decisions.text: Meeting minutes approved unanimously without modifications
Work plan approved unanimously with modification to change item 4 title to promote public art strategy
decisions.source: Jesse Cupp : All right. It passes unanimously, OK.

decisions.text: Decided against themed call for artists for 2024 CPA exhibitions, preferring open submissions
decisions.source: Jesse Cupp : We try to do a theme and people will shoehorn anything into it.

decisions.text: Submit renderings or 3D models for the transit grade separation art by July 21
decisions.source: John Lang : Five of the six artists are under contract. Sixth. We had a small issue with their W 9, which we're getting resolved, which should not impact this deliverable, but they have till July 21st either come up with renderings

decisions.text: Produce three mural concept designs each and present to the committee in September
decisions.source: John Lang : Is on rank store park, maintenance and tennis buildings. As I mentioned, agreements with Fernanda and Harumo have been executed and have asked them to complete 3 concept designs for consideration at your September meeting.

points.speaker: Jesse Cupp
points.text: Meeting called to order at 6:03 PM with roll call completed for committee members
points.source: Jesse Cupp : So this is chair Jessica on the meeting to order at six O 3:00 PM And let's go to roll call committee member Cliff Grant.

topics.topic: Public Art Inventory and Mapping Progress

points.speaker: Susi Merhar
points.text: Three additional art pieces found at El Camino Hospital through Sunnyvale's mapping website
points.source: Susi Merhar : I found on a different, I think it was City of Sunnyvale's kind of map that they're using. I looked at Mountain View on that map website and there were three more art pieces that were all in El Camino Hospital

points.speaker: John Lang
points.text: Strategy includes vision statement: 'Creation of diverse public art enriches Mountain View's innovative and creative culture, enhances community vibrancy, supports local artists and transforms Mountain View'
points.source: John Lang : The creation of diverse public art enriches mountain views innovative and creative culture, enhances the vibrancy of our community, supports local artists and transforms Mountain View into the North. That's the vision.

points.speaker: John Lang
points.text: Three main strategies identified: develop city policies for art appreciation, create more public art opportunities, and pursue collaboration with artist organizations
points.source: John Lang : So the three strategies are develop, augment, and refined city policies and procedures to better line and encourage art appreciation and placekeeping, create more public art opportunities, providing for public expression and vitality of art in the Mountain View community, and pursue new opportunities for collaboration with artist organizations.

points.speaker: John Lang
points.text: Given the heavy September workload, the committee may consider an August meeting or two September meetings. John Lang acknowledged the need to keep meetings functional and will explore options, but no scheduling decision was made at this time.
points.source: John Lang : ...happy to explore that... let's put a pin in that... I'd like to be functional

topics.topic: Public Art Inventory and Mapping Progress

points.speaker: John Lang
points.text: Next steps: John Lang will work with the City's GIS team to update the online map using the spreadsheet, explore modern ArcGIS StoryMaps and trail overlays, and consider an intake process for future updates. The current public map was last updated in 2016; publication is planned after updates.
points.source: John Lang : ...ask our GIS team... to update the... data layer... and... story maps... render that... As you heard... they've found quite a few pieces... the last time... updated was in 2016.

points.speaker: Cliff Grant
points.text: Scope considerations include whether interior works (e.g., hospital hallways) are considered public art and coordinating with El Camino Hospital, which did not respond to public inquiry. The City will engage institutions directly before publishing interior works and clarify inclusion criteria.
points.source: Cliff Grant : ...walking through El Camino... is that public art?... I wrote to them... I never got a response... John Lang : ...before we publish anything... the City can talk to El Camino.

topics.topic: Economic Impact Research for Strategy Support

points.speaker: John Lang
points.text: Staff will pair city event attendance data with conservative AEP metrics to communicate arts' value (e.g., Art & Wine Festival, performances) and support adoption of the Public Art Strategy, acknowledging updated AEP6 data will refine estimates when released.
points.source: John Lang : ...we can kind of plug this in... even just being conservative... use 2017 numbers... a way to frame the value of arts and culture... why it's time to do a public arts strategy.

points.speaker: Jesse Cupp
points.text: Suggested initiative clarifications include: explicitly treating private percent-for-art; improving visibility via guided tours and the online map; recognizing trails, easements, and rights-of-way in siting; enabling interactive and temporary art events; and including public transit routes and a maintenance plan in the utility box program.
points.source: Jesse Cupp : ...clarify... private development... improve visibility through guided tours, online map... include easements and trails... explore interactive... events... add public transit routes and a maintenance plan for utility boxes.

points.speaker: John Lang
points.text: Funding flexibility will be explored, such as whether K5 (public project) art funds can be pooled or reallocated to higher-visibility locations, while noting in-lieu payments on private projects already create a flexible pool. Legal and policy feasibility will be vetted before codifying.
points.source: John Lang : ...is there a way to take the public... K5 and move them someplace else... in-lieu... creates a pool... we need to vet this... you don't want something... if... not feasible.

points.speaker: Cliff Grant
points.text: Next step agreed: the ad hoc committee will draft revised goals (and consider any vision alignment) for full committee review in September, while staff incorporates feedback and feasibility checks. This approach aims to accelerate revisions by giving the committee a concrete draft to refine.
points.source: Cliff Grant : ...plan would be for... the ad hoc committee [to] work on a draft... we'll bring back then... review it in September.

points.speaker: John Lang
points.text: John Lang explained chat is disabled for decision-making meetings by City policy.
points.source: John Lang : ...chat is disabled... I'll send you my contact information... we should probably talk...

decisions.assignee: Visual Arts Committee Ad Hoc Committee
decisions.text: Draft revised public art goals (and consider vision alignment) for full committee discussion by the September meeting
decisions.source: Cliff Grant : ...plan would be for... the ad hoc committee [to] work on a draft... we'll bring back then... review it in September.

decisions.text: Coordinate with the GIS team to update the online public art map using the new inventory, explore StoryMaps and overlays, and prepare an updated public-facing map
decisions.source: John Lang : ...ask our GIS team... to update the... data layer... and... story maps... render that... so you have a sense of what it looks like today.

decisions.text: Pre-screen transit grade separation art submissions, then circulate to the committee for scoring and discussion at the September meeting
decisions.source: John Lang : ...my intent is to have a public works review at first... I'll ask them to screen it first. And then out to the committee.

decisions.text: Proceed with the 2024 CPA call for artists without a theme and return in October to finalize selections and align future calls to the summer schedule
decisions.source: John Lang : ...do you want a theme or just do a call for artists?... we'll come back... in October to finalize... and then it'll be... June timeframe... you'll do this again.

decisions.text: Implement the approved annual work plan change to emphasize promoting the Public Art Strategy, including updating the 1996 art book, online map, and website
decisions.source: Jesse Cupp : I will make a motion to approve the work plan with that change... It passes unanimously, OK.

decisions.text: Email Jasmin Aber to arrange an offline discussion on VAC processes and engagement
decisions.source: John Lang : I believe I have your e-mail. I'll send you my contact information... we should probably talk.

decisions.text: Staff will research feasibility of moving K5 policy funds between projects and creating alternative funding mechanisms
decisions.source: John Lang : So I put it here because I don't know the answer. Like this is good. Figure it out and see if there's a way to make it work.

decisions.text: Staff will work with GIS team to update public art map with new inventory data and explore enhanced functionality
decisions.source: John Lang : So I think what I I see personally as a next step this can continue to be a living document and will be a living document means to but I think taking what what has been generated by the subcommittee and i'm. Happy to kind of talk with our GIS team

gt1_point.text: Subcommittee will work on draft revisions to public art strategy goals and bring back to full committee in September
gt1_point.source: Susi Merhar : We'll bring it back to the city, city will make changes.

gt2_point.text: Draft revised public art goals (and consider vision alignment) for full committee discussion by the September meeting.
gt2_point.source: Cliff Grant : ...plan would be for... the ad hoc committee [to] work on a draft... we'll bring back then... review it in September.
gt2_point.assignee: Visual Arts Committee Ad Hoc Committee

needreview_gt.question: Are 'Ad hoc committee' and 'Visual Arts Committee Ad Hoc Committee' referring to the same entity for this task assignment?
needreview_gt.merge_if_valid: Use the more specific assignee name 'Visual Arts Committee Ad Hoc Committee' and merge the decision text from GT1 source

merge_stats.notes: High-quality merge with strong consensus between GTs. Most points aligned well semantically. Only one decision needs review due to assignee naming variation (likely same entity). GT1 provided more detailed strategy discussion points while GT2 had clearer meeting administration details.
